explanation: Path traversal attempting to access authentication logs.

Deployment pipeline transferring a versioned release artifact to production.
Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) payload attempting to inject a script tag to steal session cookies.

SSH configuration weakened across three critical parameters: enabling root login, enabling password auth (from key-only), and raising max auth tries to 99. This opens the server to brute force attacks and is a common post-compromise persistence technique.
ICMP echo replies with unusually large payloads in a regular pattern. This indicates ICMP tunneling being used for data exfiltration, as normal ICMP echo packets carry minimal data.
Registry persistence or credential interception technique targeting Windows authentication or logon mechanisms. The modified key enables automatic code execution during system events.
XML-RPC command execution attempt.

Kernel module loaded from a suspicious path (/tmp, /dev/shm, or hidden directory). Legitimate kernel modules are loaded from /lib/modules. Loading from temp directories indicates a rootkit installation.
System command spawned from a suspicious parent process in an unusual location, performing evidence cleanup or log clearing.
Standard application read query for published content.
SYN flood attack at 7500 packets/sec targeting port 80.
Small number of failed login attempts from an internal IP during business hours. Likely a mistyped password.

Attacker attempting to traverse directories to access sensitive system configuration files.
Writing an executable to a remote host's administrative share, a classic lateral movement technique.
Small number of failed login attempts from an internal IP during business hours. Likely a mistyped password.

Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) via webhook/callback endpoint. The API is being tricked into making requests to internal admin interfaces, potentially exposing sensitive data or enabling further attacks.
User asmith changed their own password through self-service.
Authorized administrator running a routine system command during business hours.
Certbot generating a new CSR (Certificate Signing Request) for TLS certificate renewal. The standard SSL paths and certbot executor confirm legitimate certificate lifecycle management.
Read-only AWS API call from an authorized audit role for routine cloud management.

CrowdStrike Falcon endpoint security agent being added to auto-start via MSI installer. The Program Files installation path, legitimate vendor name, and msiexec parent process confirm this is an authorized security tool deployment.
Deployment pipeline copying a versioned release artifact to production. The CI runner source, semantic versioning, and releases directory indicate a standard deployment.
Outbound connection to an external IP on a common reverse shell port, indicating possible C2 callback.
Automated TLS certificate renewal for app.company.com.

A kernel driver being dropped into the System32 drivers directory by sc.exe. This could be a rootkit installation. Legitimate driver installations go through proper Windows installer mechanisms, not direct file creation via sc.exe.
External port scan from 185.220.101.5 probing 9 ports in 1s.
Direct root login at 3:45 AM using password authentication. Root SSH login should be disabled; this suggests compromised credentials and policy violation.
SYN flood attack at 8000 packets/sec targeting port 443.
Deployment pipeline transferring a versioned release artifact to production.

Using WMI to remotely create a backdoor admin account on another host. Although executed by a deployment service account, creating users with weak passwords and adding them to administrators is not legitimate deployment behavior.

Standard application read query for pending order count.
External port scan from 77.247.181.162 probing 7 ports in 2s.
Legal team member accessing an NDA template on the file server.
On-call engineer logging in at 2 AM during an incident response. Public key auth and the oncall username indicate this is legitimate after-hours access.
SQL injection attempt using Boolean-based blind injection in the user parameter.

Rapid enumeration of common administrative and sensitive paths indicates automated vulnerability scanning or reconnaissance activity.

PowerShell AMSI bypass using reflection to set amsiInitFailed flag. The string concatenation ('Am'+'siUtils') is specifically designed to evade static detection of the AMSI tampering. This disables script-based malware scanning.
Small number of failed login attempts from an internal IP during business hours. Likely a mistyped password.
Read-only AWS API call from an authorized audit role for routine cloud management.
Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) for command execution.
Standard DNS resolution for Jenkins updates.
LOLBin execution using a signed Windows binary to proxy execution of malicious code. This technique bypasses application whitelisting and script-based defenses.
Massive volume of SYN packets attempting to exhaust server resources (Denial of Service).
Standard parameterized read query from an application to fetch active user records.
Rapid succession of failed login attempts indicating a dictionary or brute force attack.
Deployment pipeline transferring a versioned release artifact to production.

Enterprise management agent running system inventory commands. The management software parent process (ManageEngine/SolarWinds/Ivanti) and read-only system queries confirm this is authorized endpoint management.
DBA performing a production database backup using pg_dump via sudo. The output path and command are consistent with routine backup procedures.
Setting cmd.exe as the debugger for sethc.exe (Sticky Keys). This is a well-known accessibility feature backdoor that provides a SYSTEM command prompt at the login screen.

Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) via webhook/callback endpoint. The API is being tricked into making requests to internal admin interfaces, potentially exposing sensitive data or enabling further attacks.
Security operations team creating an organization-wide CloudTrail with log validation enabled. This strengthens audit capabilities and is a security best practice, not an attacker action.
High-frequency DNS TXT queries with random-looking subdomains to a suspicious domain returning NXDOMAIN. This pattern matches DNS tunneling used for data exfiltration or C2 communication.
System command spawned from a suspicious parent process in an unusual location, performing evidence cleanup or log clearing.

WPAD resolution falling through to a non-authoritative response from an unexpected host. This indicates WPAD poisoning via LLMNR/NBT-NS, allowing an attacker to intercept HTTP traffic as a man-in-the-middle proxy.
Brute force attack against postgres account with 250 attempts in 40s.

Certutil invoked by svchost.exe to verify and update the Windows root certificate trust list (AuthRoot). This is a legitimate Windows automatic root certificate update mechanism, not the -urlcache download technique used by attackers.

ptrace PTRACE_ATTACH syscall targeting PID 1 (init/systemd) from a non-root user's custom strace binary. Attaching to PID 1 enables process memory manipulation for privilege escalation or rootkit installation.
Authorized administrator running a routine system command during business hours.

Although formatted as a routine cron job, it pipes a remote script directly into bash. The internal IP 10.0.99.5 on port 8080 is not a standard update server, and piping curl to bash allows arbitrary code execution without verification.
Cross-Site Scripting attempt using iframe injection to embed a phishing page.

Trivy container vulnerability scanner performing a nightly image scan in read-only mode. Aqua Security's Trivy is a legitimate security tool, and the read-only flag confirms no write access to the host.
LOLBin execution using a signed Windows binary to proxy execution of malicious code. This technique bypasses application whitelisting and script-based defenses.
Legitimate outbound HTTP traffic permitted by firewall rule.

PowerShell profile.ps1 created/modified in the system-wide profile directory. This file executes automatically every time any user launches PowerShell, providing persistent code execution. The notepad.exe parent suggests manual attacker editing.

Kerberos TGS request using RC4-HMAC encryption for the krbtgt service. Modern environments use AES256. RC4 requests for krbtgt are a hallmark of a Golden Ticket or Kerberoasting attack.

Splunk Universal Forwarder updating its Windows Technology Add-on input configuration. The Splunk installation path and its own service process modifying its own config files is standard SIEM agent behavior.
Ransomware indicator: ransom note on admin desktop.

MSBuild executing a project file from the Public Documents folder, launched by explorer.exe (not Visual Studio). MSBuild is a known LOLBin for executing arbitrary C# code embedded in .csproj files to bypass application whitelisting.
